section.title: Jephthah's Vow
section.sectionRationale: The crisis with Ammon comes to a head, and God raises up an unlikely deliverer. Jephthah is the outcast son of a prostitute, driven from his home and gathered with worthless men, yet the elders of Gilead beg him to lead them, and the Spirit of the LORD comes upon him. He argues Israel's case with the king of Ammon in a careful history lesson, then wins a great victory. But in the flush of zeal he makes a rash vow, and it falls on his own daughter, his only child, ending in a tragedy the text records without commending. This movement holds together the grace that raises up an outcast deliverer and the folly of a leader shaped by an age when everyone did what was right in his own eyes.
section.thesis: The LORD, in sovereign grace, raises up a rejected outcast and empowers him by His Spirit to deliver Israel; yet Jephthah's rash, unbidden vow, born of a heart formed more by the surrounding paganism than by God's word, brings a needless tragedy on his own house, exposing the deep need for a righteous King whose zeal is according to knowledge.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the outcast recalled to lead, verses 1 to 11; the diplomacy with Ammon, verses 12 to 28; the Spirit, the vow, and the victory, verses 29 to 33; and the tragic homecoming and its aftermath, verses 34 to 40.

units.label: The outcast recalled to lead
units.note: the son of a prostitute becomes commander

verses.text: Now Jephthah the Gileadite was a {{gibbor|mighty man of valor}}; he was the son of a prostitute, and Gilead was his father.
And Gilead’s wife bore him sons who grew up, drove Jephthah out, and said to him, “You shall have no inheritance in our father’s house, because you are the son of another woman.”
So Jephthah fled from his brothers and settled in the land of Tob, where worthless men gathered around him and traveled with him.
Some time later, when the Ammonites fought against Israel
and made war with them, the elders of Gilead went to get Jephthah from the land of Tob.
“Come,” they said, “be our commander, so that we can fight against the Ammonites.”
Jephthah replied to the elders of Gilead, “Did you not hate me and expel me from my father’s house? Why then have you come to me now, when you are in distress?”
They answered Jephthah, “This is why we now turn to you, that you may go with us, fight the Ammonites, and become leader over all of us who live in Gilead.”
But Jephthah asked them, “If you take me back to fight the Ammonites and the LORD gives them to me, will I really be your leader?”
And the elders of Gilead said to Jephthah, “The LORD is our witness if we do not do as you say.”
So Jephthah went with the elders of Gilead, and the people made him their leader and commander. And Jephthah repeated all his terms in the presence of the LORD at Mizpah.

units.label: The diplomacy with Ammon
units.note: may the LORD, the Judge, decide today

verses.text: Then Jephthah sent messengers to the king of the Ammonites, saying, “What do you have against me that you have come to fight against my land?”

The king of the Ammonites answered Jephthah’s messengers, “When Israel came up out of Egypt, they seized my land, from the Arnon to the Jabbok and all the way to the Jordan. Now, therefore, restore it peaceably.”
Jephthah again sent messengers to the king of the Ammonites
to tell him, “This is what Jephthah says: Israel did not take away the land of Moab or of the Ammonites.
But when Israel came up out of Egypt, they traveled through the wilderness to the Red Sea and came to Kadesh.

Then Israel sent messengers to the king of Edom, saying, ‘Please let us pass through your land,’ but the king of Edom would not listen. They also sent messengers to the king of Moab, but he would not consent. So Israel stayed in Kadesh.

Then Israel traveled through the wilderness and bypassed the lands of Edom and Moab. They came to the east side of the land of Moab and camped on the other side of the Arnon. But they did not enter the territory of Moab, since the Arnon was its border.
And Israel sent messengers to Sihon king of the Amorites, who ruled in Heshbon, and said to him, ‘Please let us pass through your land into our own place.’
But Sihon would not trust Israel to pass through his territory. So he gathered all his people, encamped in Jahaz, and fought with Israel.
Then the LORD, the God of Israel, delivered Sihon and all his people into the hand of Israel, who defeated them. So Israel took possession of all the land of the Amorites who inhabited that country,
seizing all the land from the Arnon to the Jabbok and from the wilderness to the Jordan.
Now since the LORD, the God of Israel, has driven out the Amorites from before His people Israel, should you now possess it?
Do you not possess whatever your god Chemosh grants you? So also, we possess whatever the LORD our God has granted us.
Are you now so much better than Balak son of Zippor, king of Moab? Did he ever contend with Israel or fight against them?
For three hundred years Israel has lived in Heshbon, Aroer, and their villages, as well as all the cities along the banks of the Arnon. Why did you not take them back during that time?
I have not sinned against you, but you have done me wrong by waging war against me. May the LORD, the {{shaphat|Judge}}, decide today between the Israelites and the Ammonites.”
But the king of the Ammonites paid no heed to the message Jephthah sent him.

units.label: The Spirit, the vow, and the victory
units.note: whatever comes out to greet me

verses.text: Then the {{ruach|Spirit}} of the LORD came upon Jephthah, and he passed through Gilead and Manasseh, then through Mizpah of Gilead. And from there he advanced against the Ammonites.
Jephthah made this {{neder|vow}} to the LORD: “If indeed You will deliver the Ammonites into my hand,
then whatever comes out the door of my house to greet me on my triumphant return from the Ammonites will belong to the LORD, and I will offer it up as a {{olah|burnt offering}}.”
So Jephthah crossed over to the Ammonites to fight against them, and the LORD delivered them into his hand.
With a great blow he devastated twenty cities from Aroer to the vicinity of Minnith, as far as Abel-keramim. So the Ammonites were subdued before the Israelites.

units.label: The tragic homecoming
units.note: his only child came out to meet him

verses.text: And when Jephthah returned home to Mizpah, there was his daughter coming out to meet him with tambourines and dancing! She was his only child; he had no son or daughter besides her.

As soon as Jephthah saw her, he tore his clothes and said, “No! Not my daughter! You have brought me to my knees! You have brought great misery upon me, for I have given my word to the LORD and cannot take it back.”
“My father,” she replied, “you have given your word to the LORD. Do to me as you have said, for the LORD has avenged you of your enemies, the Ammonites.”
She also said to her father, “Let me do this one thing: Let me wander for two months through the mountains with my friends and mourn my virginity.”
“Go,” he said. And he sent her away for two months. So she left with her friends and mourned her virginity upon the mountains.
After two months, she returned to her father, and he did to her as he had vowed. And she had never had relations with a man. So it has become a custom in Israel
that each year the young women of Israel go out for four days to lament the daughter of Jephthah the Gileadite.

gibbor.gloss: A mighty man, warrior, man of valor. Jephthah is introduced with real honor, a capable and courageous fighter, even though he is an outcast son of a prostitute. God often raises up His deliverers from unlikely and despised places, so that the strength is plainly His gift, not the man's pedigree.

ruach.gloss: Spirit, breath, wind. The Spirit of the LORD comes upon Jephthah to equip him for battle, the recurring mark of a judge God raises up. The Spirit's empowering is for the deliverance; it does not endorse the rash vow Jephthah then adds out of his own zeal.

neder.gloss: A vow, a solemn promise made to God, often to secure His help. The law allowed vows but urged great care, since a vow once made was binding, Deuteronomy 23:21-23. Jephthah's vow is rash and unbidden; God had already given the Spirit, and no bargain was needed.

olah.gloss: A burnt offering, wholly consumed on the altar. Jephthah vows to offer up as an olah whatever comes out to greet him, language of total sacrifice. Human sacrifice was strictly forbidden and detestable to the LORD, which is why the outcome of the vow is so tragic and why interpreters have long debated exactly what happened.

shaphat.gloss: To judge, decide, govern. Jephthah appeals to the LORD, the Judge, to decide between Israel and Ammon; the same root names the book and its deliverers. It underlines that the true Judge and Ruler is God Himself, exposing Israel's need for the righteous King the book anticipates.

basic.context: Ammon is pressing hard on Gilead, and Israel has no leader. Enter Jephthah, a striking figure: a mighty warrior, but the son of a prostitute, driven from his family and living among worthless men in the land of Tob. Desperate, the same elders who cast him off now beg him to lead. Jephthah bargains, secures his position before the LORD at Mizpah, and first tries diplomacy, sending the king of Ammon a careful history lesson showing Israel's claim to the land is just. When Ammon ignores it, the Spirit of the LORD comes upon Jephthah for battle. But then, in a burst of zeal, he adds a rash vow: whatever comes out of his house to meet him on his return he will offer as a burnt offering. He wins a crushing victory, but on his return it is his only child, his daughter, who comes dancing out to meet him. He is devastated; she submits; after two months to mourn, he does to her as he had vowed. The text records the tragedy soberly and never praises the vow.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, and it is crucial to read it as description, not prescription. The Bible reports Jephthah's vow; it does not commend it. Judges is deliberately showing the decline of Israel in an age when everyone did what was right in his own eyes, and Jephthah embodies both God's grace, in raising and using him, and the tragedy of a faith shaped by pagan bargaining rather than by God's word. The Spirit came for the battle, not for the vow. We should neither excuse the vow nor use it to accuse God, who never asked for it and had forbidden human sacrifice. The passage warns against rash, superstitious religion and mourns its cost.
basic.originalLanguages: Jephthah is a gibbor, a mighty man of valor, honored despite his shameful origins. The ruach, the Spirit, of the LORD comes upon him to deliver Israel, the mark of a true judge. He appeals to the LORD as the shaphat, the Judge, to decide the case. Then he makes a neder, a vow, promising to offer as an olah, a burnt offering, whatever meets him, language of total sacrifice that human beings were never to receive, which is the root of the tragedy.
basic.typology: Jephthah, the rejected son cast out by his brothers who becomes the deliverer of the very people that spurned him, faintly foreshadows Christ, the stone the builders rejected who became the cornerstone. But the contrast is just as important: where Jephthah's rash vow brings death to his beloved only child, God the Father freely and knowingly gives His only Son, and Christ willingly offers Himself, the one acceptable burnt offering, so that we might live.
basic.christ: Jephthah is a mixed picture. As the rejected outcast raised up to save, he points to Jesus, despised and rejected, who became the deliverer of His people. Yet the sharpest gospel light falls on the daughter and the failed vow. Jephthah foolishly binds himself and it costs his only child; God, in wisdom and love, gives His only Son deliberately, and the Son consents. Where Jephthah's daughter is mourned, Christ the willing sacrifice is raised. He is the true and acceptable offering, and He is the righteous Judge and King this chapter shows Israel so badly needs.
basic.god: God is sovereign and gracious: He raises up an outcast and pours out His Spirit to save His people, showing that He delights to use the despised and that deliverance is His gift. At the same time, God is not the author of Jephthah's vow. He had forbidden human sacrifice as detestable; the folly and its cost belong to Jephthah and his age, not to the LORD. We see a God who works powerfully even through flawed servants, while the tragedy exposes how far short those servants fall and how much a righteous King is needed.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 23 warns about the seriousness of vows. Deuteronomy 12 and 18 condemn the abhorrent practice of child sacrifice. Hebrews 11 names Jephthah among those who through faith conquered kingdoms, honoring the deliverance while not endorsing the vow. And Judges 21 gives the refrain that in those days everyone did what was right in his own eyes.
basic.oneStory: God raises an unlikely outcast, fills him with His Spirit, and delivers Israel from Ammon, yet the deliverer's rash vow brings tragedy on his own house. The account holds grace and folly together and drives home the book's ache: Israel needs a Deliverer whose zeal is according to knowledge and a righteous King. That longing runs on to Christ, the rejected Son truly raised up to save, the willing and acceptable sacrifice, and the King who judges rightly.

context.literary: Jephthah's account is the centerpiece of the second half of Judges and the longest of the so-called cycles after Gideon. It is carefully structured: rejection and recall, a lengthy diplomatic argument, the Spirit and the vow, the victory, and the heartbreaking homecoming. The narrator lingers on the vow and its outcome, and the closing note about the yearly lament of Israel's young women gives the tragedy a permanent memorial. The account is framed by the theme, sounded throughout Judges, of a nation sliding downward for lack of a righteous king.
context.historical: Vows to a deity in exchange for victory were common in the ancient Near East, and so, tragically, was child sacrifice among Israel's neighbors, notably to gods like Molech and Chemosh, the very Chemosh named in verse 24. Israel had been expressly forbidden such practices, yet the pull of surrounding paganism was strong in the period of the judges. Jephthah's diplomatic speech reflects real territorial disputes east of the Jordan over lands taken from Sihon king of the Amorites; his three hundred years marks the long-settled Israelite claim.
context.canonical: The account develops the book's argument that Israel's deliverers, though genuinely raised and used by God, are increasingly compromised, pointing to the need for a king and ultimately for the Messiah. Jephthah's appeal to the LORD as Judge, and the recurring refrain about everyone doing right in his own eyes, tie this movement to the book's theology. The New Testament honors Jephthah's faith in the deliverance, Hebrews 11:32, without endorsing his vow, distinguishing the grace of God at work in a man from the man's own folly.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show both the sovereign grace of God, who raises up a despised outcast and empowers him by His Spirit to deliver Israel, and the tragic folly of a leader shaped by his pagan age, whose rash vow brings needless grief, so that the reader feels the deepening need for a righteous king whose zeal is guided by God's word.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is the crucial interpretive key. The passage describes Jephthah's vow and its outcome; it does not prescribe it or hold it up as an example to follow. Scripture reports the tragedy honestly, and the wider law of God expressly forbids human sacrifice. The abiding lessons are the danger of rash, bargaining religion, the seriousness of vows, God's grace toward the outcast, and the need for the righteous King the book anticipates. What Jephthah did wrong is recorded as a warning, not a model.

debates.question: What actually happened to Jephthah's daughter, death or lifelong dedication?

views.label: She was put to death as a burnt offering
views.case: The plain sense of the vow, to offer up as a burnt offering, and the words he did to her as he had vowed, point to a literal, tragic sacrifice; the narrator records it as the horrifying fruit of a rash vow in a dark age, without approving it, which fits the book's downward spiral and its refusal to whitewash its heroes.

views.label: She was dedicated to perpetual virginity in the LORD's service
views.case: The text stresses that she mourned her virginity, not her death, and says she never had relations with a man; on this reading the vow was fulfilled by giving her wholly to the LORD in lifelong celibacy, a living dedication rather than a killing, which some argue better fits a Spirit-empowered judge and the horror of human sacrifice in Israel.

debates.bearing: Either way, the vow was rash and needless, the tragedy real, and the narrator withholds all praise; the passage warns against superstitious bargaining with God rather than commending Jephthah's action, and it deepens the longing for a righteous deliverer.

debates.question: Does the Spirit coming upon Jephthah endorse his vow?

views.label: The Spirit empowered the deliverance, not the vow
views.case: The Spirit comes in verse 29 to equip Jephthah for battle, the standard pattern for a judge; the vow in verses 30-31 is Jephthah's own addition, a needless bargain made after God had already granted His Spirit, and it carries no divine sanction.

views.label: The vow reveals the flawed heart of a Spirit-used man
views.case: The same man the Spirit empowers still thinks like his pagan age, imagining he must strike a deal to secure a victory God was already giving; the juxtaposition shows that God's genuine use of a person does not guarantee the wisdom or godliness of all they do.

debates.bearing: Both readings free God from authoring the vow and press the point that Spirit-empowered service must still be governed by God's word, not by superstition or self-made bargains.

study.originalLanguages: The vocabulary carries the whole drama. Jephthah is a gibbor, a mighty man of valor, honored for courage even though he is the despised son of a prostitute, a reminder that God raises deliverers from unlikely places. He appeals to the LORD as the shaphat, the Judge, to decide the case between Israel and Ammon, the very root that names the book and every deliverer in it, quietly insisting that the true Judge is God. Then the ruach, the Spirit, of the LORD comes upon him, the mark of a God-raised judge, equipping him for battle. But after the Spirit, and needlessly, Jephthah makes a neder, a vow, a binding promise to God that the law urged great care in making, and he promises to offer up whatever meets him as an olah, a burnt offering, wholly consumed on the altar. That word olah is the tragedy in a nutshell, for human beings were never to be offered to the LORD; child sacrifice was detestable to Him. The Spirit came for the deliverance; the vow came from Jephthah's own zeal, and the words themselves mark the line between what God gave and what a man in a dark age foolishly added.

typology.type: The rejected outcast raised up to deliver his people
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the stone the builders rejected who became the cornerstone and the Savior of those who spurned Him, Psalm 118:22 and 1 Peter 2:4-7.

typology.type: The only child given up, set against Jephthah's rash vow
typology.fulfillment: The Father who willingly and knowingly gives His only Son, and the Son who freely consents, John 3:16 and Romans 8:32.

typology.type: The burnt offering language of total sacrifice
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the one willing and acceptable offering who gives Himself for us, Ephesians 5:2 and Hebrews 10:10.

typology.type: The appeal to the LORD as Judge
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the righteous Judge and King the book longs for, who judges the world in righteousness, Acts 17:31.

study.christ: Jephthah is a study in contrasts that all point to Christ. As the rejected son, cast out by his brothers and then made the savior of the very people who spurned him, he foreshadows Jesus, despised and rejected, who became the deliverer of His own. Yet the deepest gospel note sounds in the failed vow. Jephthah foolishly and needlessly binds himself, and it costs the life of his beloved only child, whom the daughters of Israel then mourn. Over against that stands the Father who, in perfect wisdom and love, deliberately gives His only Son, and the Son who willingly offers Himself as the true burnt offering, wholly given for us. Where Jephthah's daughter is lamented, Christ the acceptable sacrifice is raised in triumph. And Jephthah's own cry to the LORD as Judge points beyond every flawed judge of the book to the one righteous Judge and King, Jesus, whose zeal is always according to knowledge and whose deliverance never ends in needless grief.
study.god: God is shown to be both gloriously gracious and utterly holy. He is gracious in raising up Jephthah at all, an outcast son of a prostitute, and in pouring out His Spirit to deliver Israel through him, proof that God delights to save through the despised and that the victory is His gift, not the deliverer's merit. But God is not the author of the vow. He had plainly forbidden human sacrifice as an abomination, and He asked no bargain from Jephthah; the folly and its cost belong to the man and his pagan-shaped age. So the chapter guards two truths at once: God works powerfully, even through deeply flawed servants, and yet those servants fall far short, so that the story itself cries out for a righteous King whose obedience is whole and whose zeal is wise.

crossReferences.note: The seriousness of vows to the LORD; it is better not to vow than to vow rashly, the very danger Jephthah falls into.
The LORD detests the nations' practice of burning their sons and daughters as offerings; human sacrifice was strictly forbidden in Israel.
Jephthah is named among those who through faith conquered kingdoms, honoring the deliverance while not endorsing the vow.
In those days there was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in his own eyes, the refrain that frames Jephthah's tragedy.
God did not spare His own Son but gave Him up for us all, the deliberate, loving gift set against Jephthah's rash vow.

study.oneStory: God raises up a rejected outcast, fills him with His Spirit, and delivers Israel from Ammon, and yet the same deliverer's rash, needless vow brings tragedy on his own only child. The account will not flatten either truth: God's grace is real, and the folly of a leader shaped by his pagan age is real too. Set within a book whose refrain is that everyone did what was right in his own eyes, Jephthah's story deepens the ache for a Deliverer whose zeal is according to knowledge and for a righteous King. That ache runs on through Scripture to Christ, the rejected Son truly raised up to save, the willing and acceptable sacrifice given by the Father, and the King who judges the world in perfect righteousness.

quiz.q: Who was Jephthah, and how did he come to lead Israel?

options.text: A mighty warrior and outcast son of a prostitute, whom the desperate elders of Gilead begged to be their commander.
options.note: Verses 1-11 show the rejected outcast recalled to lead against Ammon.

options.text: A respected son of Gilead who was the natural heir to leadership.
options.note: He was a son of Gilead and a mighty man, but he was driven out and denied any inheritance.

options.text: A foreign general hired by Israel to fight Ammon.
options.note: He was a Gileadite Israelite, not a foreign mercenary, though he had been an outcast.

options.text: A priest appointed by lot to lead the tribes.
options.note: Jephthah was a warrior raised up as a judge, not a priest chosen by lot.

quiz.q: How did Jephthah first try to deal with the Ammonite threat?

options.text: By diplomacy, sending the king a careful history showing Israel's just claim to the land.
options.note: Verses 12-27 record his reasoned appeal before any battle, ending with the LORD the Judge.

options.text: By immediately attacking without any negotiation.
options.note: He did fight later, but he first attempted a lengthy diplomatic argument.

options.text: By paying tribute to buy off the Ammonites.
options.note: He offered no tribute; he argued the justice of Israel's cause.

options.text: By surrendering the disputed land to keep peace.
options.note: The opposite: he defended Israel's rightful possession of the land.

quiz.q: What was Jephthah's rash vow, and when did he make it?

options.text: After the Spirit came on him, he vowed to offer as a burnt offering whatever came out of his house to meet him.
options.note: Verses 29-31 show the needless vow added after God had already given His Spirit.

options.text: He vowed to build an altar to the LORD if he won.
options.note: A vow was made, but it was to offer up whatever met him, not to build an altar.

options.text: He vowed to give a tenth of the spoils to the priests.
options.note: The vow was far graver, promising a burnt offering, not a tithe of spoil.

options.text: He made no vow at all before the battle.
options.note: He explicitly made a vow to the LORD in verses 30-31.

quiz.q: How should we read the vow and its outcome in this account?

options.text: As a tragedy the text describes but never commends, the fruit of rash, pagan-shaped religion.
options.note: Judges reports the vow honestly as description, not prescription; God forbade human sacrifice.

options.text: As a costly but praiseworthy act of devotion to God.
options.note: The narrator withholds all praise; the vow was rash and needless, not a model of devotion.

options.text: As proof that God demands such vows from His servants.
options.note: God demanded no such vow and had forbidden human sacrifice; the folly was Jephthah's.

options.text: As something God commanded Jephthah to do.
options.note: God never commanded it; He detests child sacrifice, Deuteronomy 12:31.

quiz.q: Why has there long been debate about what happened to Jephthah's daughter?

options.text: Because the text stresses her virginity and never having a husband, so some read a literal sacrifice and others a lifelong dedication to the LORD.
options.note: Both readings agree the vow was rash and the tragedy real; the details leave the exact outcome debated.

options.text: Because the text clearly says she escaped unharmed.
options.note: The text does not say she escaped; it says he did to her as he had vowed.

options.text: Because different manuscripts give completely different endings.
options.note: The debate is over interpreting the same text, not over conflicting manuscripts.

options.text: Because the daughter is never mentioned again anywhere.
options.note: She is remembered yearly by the young women of Israel, verse 40.

quiz.q: How does Jephthah's story point us toward Christ?

options.text: As a rejected outcast raised up to save, and by contrast in the only child given up, pointing to the Son the Father willingly gave.
options.note: Jephthah foreshadows the rejected Savior; the failed vow contrasts with God's deliberate gift of His Son.

options.text: By showing that God's deliverers are always sinless examples.
options.note: The opposite; Jephthah's flaws show the need for the truly righteous King, Christ.

options.text: By teaching that rash vows are the path to God's favor.
options.note: The vow brought tragedy, not favor; it is a warning, not a path to follow.

options.text: By proving Jephthah himself was the promised Messiah.
options.note: Jephthah is a flawed foreshadowing, not the Messiah; he points beyond himself to Christ.

questions: God raised up Jephthah, a despised outcast, to deliver His people. Where might God be at work through people you are tempted to overlook or write off?
The Spirit came for the battle, but the rash vow was Jephthah's own addition. Are you tempted to bargain with God for what He is already willing to give by grace?
Jephthah's zeal was real but not guided by God's word, and it cost him dearly. Where do you need your zeal to be shaped by Scripture rather than by feeling or the culture around you?
The account records a tragedy without excusing it. How does it help your faith that the Bible tells the truth about its heroes' failures rather than covering them up?
Jephthah's only child was lost through a foolish vow; God gave His only Son deliberately and in love. How does that contrast move you to worship Christ, the willing and acceptable sacrifice?

translationNotes.note: Mighty man of valor renders gibbor, a term of real honor for a capable warrior; it stands in deliberate tension with the shameful note that he was the son of a prostitute.
Chemosh was the national god of Moab and is here associated with Ammon; Jephthah argues from Ammon's own worldview, not endorsing Chemosh but pressing the logic of their claim.

Whatever comes out the door of my house can be read as whoever, and offer it up as a burnt offering renders olah, wholly consumed; the wording is central to the long debate over the vow's fulfillment.
He did to her as he had vowed, followed by the note that she never had relations with a man, is read by some as a literal sacrifice and by others as lifelong dedication to the LORD in virginity.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Judges 11:1-40, all 40 verses. Verses 16, 38, and 39 concatenate their string entries around footnote and line-break markers. The vow is handled as description, not prescription; the interpretive debate over the daughter's fate is presented with the strongest case for each view. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. Ordinary hyphens retained in place names such as Abel-keramim. Sermon status none: no verified expository sermon on this specific movement located this session.
